        transform: translateY(-10px);
        box-shadow: 0 20px 30px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
    }
    .number {
        font-weight: bold;
        white-space: nowrap;
        overflow: hidden;
        text-overflow: ellipsis;
        max-width: 90%;
    }
    .title {
        font-size: 1em;
        margin-top: 10px;
        color: #e0e0e0;
    }
    /* Rainbow colors for cards */
    .rainbow-0 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ff6b6b, #f06595); }
    .rainbow-1 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #feca57, #ff9f43); }
    .rainbow-2 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1dd1a1, #10ac84); }
    .rainbow-3 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #54a0ff, #2e86de); }
    .rainbow-4 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #5f27cd, #341f97); }
    .rainbow-5 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ee5253, #c0392b); }
    .rainbow-6 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #48dbfb, #00d2d3); }
</style>
<script>
    // Adjust font size based on length
    function adjustFontSize() {
        const numbers = document.querySelectorAll('.number');
        numbers.forEach(num => {
            const length = num.innerText.length;
            if(length <= 5) num.style.fontSize = '2.5em';
            else if(length <= 8) num.style.fontSize = '2em';
            else num.style.fontSize = '1.5em';
        });
    }
    window.onload = adjustFontSize;
</script>
</head>
<body>
<h1>SAP Analytics Cloud - RESTAPI Fetched Sales KPI Dashboard</h1>
<div class="container">
    {% for kpi in kpis %}
    <div class="card rainbow-{{ loop.index0 % 7 }}">
        <div class="number">{{ kpi.number }}</div>
        <div class="title">{{ kpi.title }}</div>
    </div>
    {% endfor %}
</div>
</body>
</html>
"""
from flask import Flask, render_template_string
import requests
import urllib.parse
import webbrowser

# ---------------- CONFIG ----------------
TENANT_URL = "https://yourtenant.hanacloudservices.cloud.sap"
CLIENT_ID = "<YOUR_CLIENT_ID>"
CLIENT_SECRET = "<YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET>"
AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = "https://yourtenant.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/authorize"
TOKEN_ENDPOINT = "https://yourtenant.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
REDIRECT_URI = "https://your-app-domain.com/oauth/callback"

STORY_ID = "<your_storyid>"
WIDGET_IDS = [
    "Chart_1", "Chart_2", "Chart_3",
    "Chart_4", "Chart_5", "Chart_6", "Chart_8"
]

# ---------------- FLASK APP ----------------
app = Flask(__name__)

def get_access_token():
    # Step 1: login manually
    params = {"response_type": "code", "client_id": CLIENT_ID, "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI}
    auth_url = AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)
    print("\nOpen this URL in browser to login to SAC:")
    print(auth_url)
    webbrowser.open(auth_url)
    code = input("\nPaste the authorization code here: ").strip()

    # Step 2: get token
    payload = {
        "grant_type": "authorization_code",
        "code": code,
        "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI,
        "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET
    }
    r = requests.post(TOKEN_ENDPOINT, data=payload, headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"})
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["access_token"]

def fetch_kpis(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Accept": "application/json"}
    kpis = []
    for widget_id in WIDGET_IDS:
        url = f"{TENANT_URL}/widgetquery/getWidgetData"
        params = {"storyId": STORY_ID, "widgetId": widget_id, "type": "kpiTile"}
        r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
        if r.ok:
            data = r.json()
            kpis.append({
                "title": data.get("title", widget_id),
                "number": data.get("number", "N/A")
            })
        else:
            kpis.append({"title": widget_id, "number": "Error"})
    return kpis
# HTML Code will be written Here
@app.route("/")
def dashboard():
    token = get_access_token()
    kpis = fetch_kpis(token)
    return render_template_string(HTML_TEMPLATE, kpis=kpis)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True,port=8000)

 An explanation of the code

  1. /route logic
    • To manually retrieve an OAuth token, use get_access_token().
    • Uses fetch_kpis() to retrieve KPI tile data.
    • Uses the HTML_TEMPLATE to render all KPIs with rainbow-colored cards.
  2. Managing OAuth (get_access_token)
    • Launches the browser's SAC login.
    • The authorization code is pasted by the user. To call SAC REST APIs, code is exchanged for access tokens.
  3. Data retrieval for widgets (fetch_kpis)
    • Repeats over every WIDGET_IDS.
    • For every widget, the widgetquery/getWidgetData endpoint is called.
    • Gathers the number and title for the display.
  4. Using HTML Templates for UI rendering
    • Flex arrangement is used for tiles.
    • Each KPI card has a rainbow gradient background.
    • For a contemporary appearance, use shadow and hover effects.
    • The length of the integer automatically modifies the font size.

Result/Output

The KPI data is retrieved and shown in a personalized web dashboard after the program has launched and the user has successfully logged in using SAP Analytics Cloud OAuth.

SAP Analytics Cloud Story KPI Tiles

The original KPI tiles as they appear in the SAP Analytics Cloud narrative are depicted in the image below. Business users in SAC are in charge of configuring and maintaining these KPIs.

 

Custom Web Dashboard with KPI Tiles

The widgetquery/getWidgetData REST API is used to retrieve the same KPI values, which are then shown in a specially created web application. Custom Web Dashboard Screenshot

Important Findings

The KPI numbers in the SAC story and the web dashboard are same.
Every KPI tile shows:

  1. Value or Number 
  2. The title

The website's dashboard uses the following to improve visualization:

  1. Gradient cards with rainbows
  2. Hover animations and shadows
  3. Adaptable design

This proves to the secure consumption and reuse of SAC KPI data outside of the SAC user interface without requiring the duplication of business logic.

Conclusion

In this blog post, we shown a simple yet effective technique for extracting SAP REST APIs are used to tile Analytics Cloud KPI data and display it on a special webpage.

Important findings:

  • SAC KPIs can be consumed using custom dashboards outside of the SAC UI.

  • Python provides an adaptable and lightweight backend for API integration. REST APIs enable KPI tracking in real-time, while front-end. Style increases visibility.

  • Setting up the environment and security is essential for a safe execution.

  • SAC insights can be incorporated into executive dashboards, intranet portals, or external web apps, businesses are able to give users a consolidated view of key performance indicators without having to Launch SAC directly.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
entry.content:
Introduction

In Q4 2025, we released live versions for planning models in SAP Analytics Cloud: 

  • In seamless planning, you can connect live to fact data in SAP Datasphere (blogpost)
  • In non-seamless planning scenarios, you can connect to the live SQL sources available in SAP Analytics Cloud. Up until now, Google BigQuery was the only available source (blogpost)

With QRC1 2026, Snowflake is added to the portfolio of live sources and with that, you can now reference Snowflake data for planning without replication!

 

How does it work?

In my blogpost on live versions from Google BigQuery, I demonstrated how to add live data to an existing model. So this time, we will take another approach and create a planning model from data.

  • Go to the modeler
  • Create model
  • Create model from data
  • Choose Snowflake. You can filter the list of connections to Data Storage “Cloud”
  • Choose a connection.
  • Choose a view
  • The model is created along the source view’s structures. You see the instant live connection in the data preview

Model creation from live data

You can now work with this model as you know from model’s without live versions. In the example below, we remove dimensions and measures that we do not need. We re-create one of the measures as a live calculation.

Calculations on live data

We can also leverage the data in data actions. See this example where we copy the Actual data into a plan version with a factor. The actual version that you see is sourced directly from Snowflake. When we copy the data into a plan version, the plan data is stored in the fact table of our model in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

Planning

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (=not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view?

Yes. However, not all data types are supported (details).

What can I do with live versions? (And what can I not do?)

  • You can display the live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, etc. in stories.
  • You can reference the live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions (incl. advanced formulas).
  • You cannot write back to the source. You can copy the live version data into planning versions via copy/paste in the table, data actions and version management.

How do I see if data is live or not?

  • In models, a live version is highlighted in the data preview. In the version dimension, you see the data source per version. 
  • In tables, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted accordingly. Reasons for unplannable data give accurate information.

 

Conclusion

Live data access in planning models represents a massive opportunity. SAP Analytics Cloud aligns with SAP Business Data Cloud’s ambition to be open and keeps on expanding its live connectivity portfolio. Look forward to use your Azure data for planning in the future and follow the respective innovation in the Roadmap Explorer.

entry.title: SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM - Creating a connection with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise [SAC]
entry.content:
SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 (V2) and Enterprise Service Management (ESM) deliver modern, modular customer‑experience capabilities. But as organizations scale, the real differentiator becomes analytics—the ability to unify sales, service, and enterprise operations into a single insights layer.

SAP provides two major analytics options:

  1. Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (eSAC) – included and built into V2 and ESM
  2. SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise Edition – the full standalone analytics platform

Pre-requisities

  • Create SAP Technical Support ticket Component to choose (CEC-CRM-ANA) to enable Enterprise live connectivity
  • SSO must be enabled in both systems (SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2 & SAP Analytics Cloud)
  • Login via SSO in Sales and Service Cloud v2 Application.
  • Login to Enterprise SAC using the same SSO as used above.
  • Now try to create the connection and it should be successful (shown in below steps - screenshots).

Before you proceed to Steps : Note : While doing handshake - Login to both system should be in same browser but in different tabs.

Step by Step via Video :  Setting up the Connection

Step 1 : SSO must be enabled in order to work both Applications ( SSC v2 & SAC)

Step 2 : Login to SAP Analytics Cloud (Enterprise License version) and you must be Administrator to config

Step 3 : Select Connect to Live Data - Select a Data Source - SAP Cloud Application

Step 4 : Select from dropdown SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2

Step 5 : Enter the Application URL of your SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 URL then immediately the Redirection happens to authenticate against both systems if the same IDP matches with successfull response.

Application URL - https://<tenant-url>/sap/c4c/api/v1/ana-story-service 

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise unlocks powerful, cross-functional insights. While eSAC provides rapid, embedded visualization, SAC Enterprise enables enterprise-scale reporting and combines data from multiple systems.

For organizations seeking lighter setups, custom integration patterns using embedded Analytics within v2, using V2 REST APIs and BTP or SAP Databricks are viable alternatives.

By following the steps and architecture options outlined in this guide, your organization can create a unified analytics environment that empowers sales, service, and enterprise teams with real-time, actionable insights.

entry.title: Support of a Compact Display for Tables (Live BW)
entry.content:
Introduction

As part of our continuous innovation for Live BW connectivity, we are introducing Compact Display for Tables with the 2026 Q1 QRC release.

Compact Display is designed to simplify complex table structures by consolidating multiple dimensions into a single, structured hierarchy within a row or column.

The goal is to enhance readability, optimize space usage, and improve analytical clarity, especially in enterprise scenarios with deep dimensional breakdowns.

For customers familiar with the “Universal Display Hierarchy” in SAP BEx Query Designer, Compact Display delivers a similar conceptual benefit in SAC’s Optimized Experience, while being purpose-built for SAC architecture and modern UX standards.

 

What is the Feature “Compact Display”?

Compact Display allows you to group multiple dimensions on a row or column axis into a single structured display, enabling expandable nodes instead of separate stacked dimensions.

What it does

When activated on an axis:

  • All dimensions bound to that axis are grouped into one single row or column structure
  • The structure follows the top-to-bottom order of entities bound to the axis
  • Users can expand or collapse nodes interactively
  • The header displays the names of the compacted Dimensions/Measures
  • Display Attributes are not compacted and remain as separate rows/columns

What it does not do

  • If the axis contains only measures, Compact Display:
    • Is unchecked
    • Is disabled
    • Displays the tooltip:
      "You can't apply compact display because this axis lacks dimensions or contains only measures."
  • If no dimensions or measures are bound to the axis, the option is disabled.
  • Compact Display does nothing when only measures exist on the axis.

Why it matters

In real-life enterprise reporting scenarios, tables can quickly become:

  • Wide and hard to read
  • Vertically heavy
  • Difficult to navigate

Compact Display helps you to:

  • Improve density without losing structure
  • Enhance hierarchical readability
  • Provide a cleaner user experience

 

How to Use It

Compact Display is available in the Optimized Experience. If not yet enabled, you must opt-in to the “New Table Builder Experience”.

Step-by-Step Activation

  1. Open your story
  2. Select your Table
  3. In the Builder Panel, locate the Row or Column axis
  4. Click the “…” menu
  5. Select Compact Display

Behavior logic

Scenario

Compact Display Option

No real dimension on axis (only measures or empty)

Disabled, unchecked, tooltip shown

At least one dimension added

Enabled; any new dimension or measure added to the axis will be added to the Compact Display

Last real dimension removed

Disabled again, unchecked, tooltip shown

 

See It in Action

 

Share Your Feedback

Compact Display is an enhancement for Live BW scenarios, and your feedback is essential.

It directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please share your thoughts and experience through:

  • SAP Community discussions
  • Customer Influence channels

Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: SAP Destination Service Troubleshooting
entry.content:
Destination Service Authentication Troubleshooting Made Simple: OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion Guide 

Troubleshoot like a pro with REST API validation! 

Configuration issues in OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion destinations can be tricky to diagnose. Destination Service: Troubleshooting Guide shows you a systematic approach using the Destination Service REST API to pinpoint errors quickly: 

We'll use a SuccessFactors integration example, but the same principles work for any target system using OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion or can be tested for other authentication types like SAMLAssertion. 

Understanding the Problem:  

When an integration fails, it’s important to pinpoint the root cause. Is the issue with the destination setup? User authentication? Token exchange? Rather than testing through your application which adds unnecessary complexity you can directly verify the destination by calling the Destination Service REST API.

This method separates the connectivity layer from your application logic. If you successfully obtain an access token, your destination is properly configured, meaning any remaining problems lie in your application code or the target system not in BTP connectivity.

What You'll Need Before Starting: 

Make sure you have Administrator access to your BTP subaccount, Space Manager or Developer permissions, a configured OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion destination, a REST client like Bruno or Postman, and verify that your test user exists in both your Identity Provider and the target system. 

For a complete step by step guide on how to achieve this check following KBA: 3708599 - Troubleshooting Guide for scenarios in BTP where Destination service is involved. 

Save this guide for faster troubleshooting, clearer error diagnosis, and confident destination configuration!

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Go-Live Success: A Comprehensive Checklist for Driving User Adoption
entry.content:
Implementing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) successfully requires more than just technical deployment—it demands a strategic approach to ensure optimal performance, user adoption, and long-term value. This comprehensive guide outlines the critical areas organizations must address to achieve a successful SAC go-live for both Analytics and Planning scenarios.

The Foundation: Four Pillars of SAC Success

A successful SAC implementation rests on four fundamental pillars that must be carefully evaluated and optimized:

  1. Data Acquisition and Live Connection
  2. Modeling Excellence
  3. Story Design Optimization
  4. Ongoing House Keeping

Let's explore each pillar in detail to understand how they contribute to driving adoption and consumption in your SAC environment.

Pillar 1: Data Acquisition and Live Connection

The foundation of any analytics solution lies in robust data connectivity. For SAC implementations, this means ensuring your infrastructure can handle the demands of real-time analytics and planning scenarios.

Network and Infrastructure Considerations

Your network configuration becomes critical, especially when implementing live connections to source systems. This includes:

  • Device compatibility testing across laptops and mobile devices to ensure consistent user experience
  • Source system capacity evaluation for live scenarios to prevent performance bottlenecks
  • Tenant capacity planning, particularly crucial for import scenarios and planning workloads

End-to-End Connection Validation

A comprehensive review of your SAC connections should cover multiple technical layers:

  • Cloud Connector configuration and optimization
  • Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO) setup
  • User authorization frameworks
  • Browser behavior testing in realistic business scenarios

These elements work together to create a seamless user experience that encourages adoption rather than creating barriers.

Pillar 2: Modeling Excellence

Effective modeling in SAC requires balancing functionality with performance, ensuring your solution serves actual business needs rather than simply showcasing available data.

Requirement Alignment and Simplification

Before diving into complex modeling scenarios, evaluate whether your requirements align with SAC's standard capabilities. Ask yourself:

  • Do your needs fit SAC's out-of-the-box functionality?
  • Are you creating unnecessary workarounds for complex workflows?
  • Is your commenting and large table handling approach sustainable?

Business-Focused Design Philosophy

The most successful SAC implementations follow a principle of designing for the business goal rather than building from all available data. This approach involves:

  • Keeping models simple and efficient by using only truly necessary data
  • Reducing data volume early in the modeling process
  • Avoiding unnecessary complexity that can hinder maintenance and scaling
  • Designing for maintainability to ensure long-term success

SAC Best Practices Implementation

Following established SAC best practices ensures your solution is secure, reliable, and provides smooth workflows. Key areas include:

  • Proper date handling and hierarchy design
  • Robust access control implementation
  • Comprehensive validation frameworks
  • Optimized planning area configuration

Pillar 3: Story Design Optimization

User experience in SAC stories directly impacts adoption rates. Poor performance or clunky interfaces can quickly derail even the most well-intentioned analytics initiatives.

Script and Event Optimization

Pay special attention to story pages utilizing scripts in critical events:

  • Review "onInitialization" and "onActive" event implementations
  • Eliminate unnecessary widget refreshes that can slow down user interactions
  • Optimize long-running data actions and multi-actions for better performance

Performance Testing and Validation

Comprehensive performance testing should cover both individual and concurrent usage scenarios:

  • Single-user performance optimization to ensure responsive individual experiences
  • High user concurrency testing to validate system behavior under realistic load conditions
  • Realistic scenario and volume testing to identify potential bottlenecks before go-live

Pillar 4: House Keeping and Ongoing Maintenance

Successful SAC implementations require ongoing attention to system health and optimization opportunities.

System Monitoring and Health Checks

Regular monitoring of your SAC environment should include:

  • Homepage System Overview reviews to identify potential issues early
  • Import job failure monitoring to ensure data freshness and reliability
  • Data action performance tracking to identify optimization opportunities
  • Long-running query analysis to prevent user experience degradation

Job Optimization and Scheduling

Efficient job management contributes significantly to overall system performance:

  • Review calendar job cadence regularly
  • Adjust or reduce job frequency where possible without impacting business requirements
  • Balance system resource utilization across different time periods

Driving Adoption Through Excellence

The ultimate goal of following this comprehensive checklist is to create an SAC environment that users want to engage with regularly. When data connections are reliable, models are intuitive, stories perform well, and the system runs smoothly, user adoption naturally follows.

Organizations that systematically address each of these four pillars typically see higher user engagement, better business outcomes, and stronger return on their SAC investment. The key is treating go-live not as a destination, but as the beginning of an ongoing optimization journey.

Next Steps for Your SAC Implementation

As you prepare for your SAC go-live or optimize an existing implementation, consider conducting a comprehensive review across all four pillars. Start with the areas that most directly impact your users' daily experience, then work systematically through the technical infrastructure elements that support those experiences. If you seek expert services and guidance for any of the areas please check our engagement plans and service portfolio.

Remember, successful SAC adoption is built on the foundation of technical excellence, user-centric design, and ongoing operational discipline. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on these core principles, your organization can maximize the value of its SAC investment while driving meaningful business outcomes through enhanced analytics and planning capabilities.
